findings.description: The callout claims 'O(n) = O(n^2) is true (every linear function is also quadratic-bounded)' — while the containment O(n) ⊆ O(n²) is true, writing O(n) = O(n²) as an equation is precisely the kind of false equality the callout is warning against, making the example self-contradictory and misleading; the correct statement is O(n) ⊆ O(n²), not O(n) = O(n²).
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'O(n) = O(n^2) is true' with 'O(n) ⊆ O(n^2) is true' to correctly express the containment relationship.

findings.description: The display-math block defining the limit L is immediately followed by three bullet blocks that explicitly interpret the cases of that limit, but the display-math block and the bullets do not share a group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id (e.g., 'limit-table') to block 20d7bbac and the three following bullet blocks af99166a, 7a0f7e21, and 53d5be7a.

findings.description: The callout block [34c2d373] formally states the max-heap property formula H[i] ≤ H[parent(i)] immediately after the paragraph [97a1c334] that defines the property in words, but the two blocks share no group_id despite the callout directly formalizing the preceding paragraph's definition.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks 97a1c334 and 34c2d373.

findings.description: The HeapifyUp pseudocode block [809cf8cc] is immediately followed by a paragraph [7f624dd0] that walks through a specific execution of that exact pseudocode, but the two blocks share no group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks 809cf8cc and 7f624dd0.

findings.description: The HeapifyDown pseudocode block [75d14787] is immediately followed by a paragraph [0802541f] that explains the logic of choosing the larger child and the complexity, directly interpreting the pseudocode, but the two blocks share no group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks 75d14787 and 0802541f.

findings.description: The BuildHeap pseudocode block [3a02f8ab] is immediately followed by a paragraph [9b0057c8] that explains why the total work is O(n) rather than O(n log n), directly interpreting that algorithm, but the two blocks share no group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks 3a02f8ab and 9b0057c8.

findings.description: The display-math block for the worst-case recurrence is immediately followed by a paragraph (36a7ebe8) that explicitly interprets that equation and explains the unrolling, but the two blocks share no group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks c6e45aa2 and 36a7ebe8 so they are rendered as a coupled unit.

findings.description: The display-math block for the 10%/90% unbalanced-split recurrence is immediately followed by a paragraph (180e791d) that explicitly interprets its depth and cost, but the two blocks share no group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks bd449880 and 180e791d.

findings.description: The expression array ["x^3", "x^(log(7)/log(2))"] uses log() which in mathjs defaults to the natural logarithm, so log(7)/log(2) correctly computes log₂7 — however, the renderer for function2d expects a single expression string, not an array; passing an array literal as the expression is likely to fail or be misinterpreted.
findings.suggested_fix: If the renderer supports only a single expression, split into two separate plot blocks or confirm the renderer accepts an array; if arrays are supported, verify the syntax matches the renderer's API.

findings.description: The block states C₂₂ = M₁ − M₂ + M₃ + M₆, but the correct Strassen formula is C₂₂ = M₁ − M₂ + M₄ + M₆ (M₄, not M₃); M₃ = A₁₁(B₁₂ − B₂₂) does not contribute to C₂₂.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the C₂₂ formula in the prose (and in the matching display-math block id=339d3529) to M₁ − M₂ + M₄ + M₆.

findings.description: The display-math gives C₂₂ = M₁ − M₂ + M₃ + M₆, but the correct Strassen reconstruction is C₂₂ = M₁ − M₂ + M₄ + M₆.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace M₃ with M₄ in the C₂₂ line of the display-math block.

findings.description: The pseudocode line 'C22 = M1 - M2 + M3 + M6' repeats the same error: C₂₂ should use M₄, not M₃.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'C22 = M1 - M2 + M3 + M6' to 'C22 = M1 - M2 + M4 + M6' in the pseudocode.

findings.description: The callout states '|E| ≤ |V|²' for a simple graph, but for a simple undirected graph the correct bound is |E| ≤ |V|(|V|−1)/2, and for a simple directed graph |E| ≤ |V|(|V|−1); neither equals |V|² because self-loops are excluded.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '|E| ≤ |V|²' with '|E| ≤ |V|(|V|−1)/2' for undirected simple graphs, or note the directed bound separately.

findings.description: The display-math states a biconditional (⟺) between the interval nesting condition and 'w is an ancestor of v', but the source chunks state this as a one-directional fact ('If vertex w is an ancestor of vertex v … then pre[w] < pre[v] < post[v] < post[w]'); the converse is true in DFS trees but the source does not assert it as a biconditional, and more importantly the generated block's biconditional conflates the ancestor relationship with the tree/forward edge classification (which also requires an actual edge), making the standalone claim potentially misleading.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the biconditional (⟺) to a one-directional implication (⟹) to match the source fact: 'if w is an ancestor of v in the DFS tree, then pre[w] < pre[v] < post[v] < post[w]'.

findings.description: The block claims there are 'exactly three cases' for edge classification in a directed graph, but the source chunks list three interval patterns covering tree/forward, back, and cross edges without claiming these are exhaustive of all possible interval relationships; more importantly, in a directed graph DFS there are actually four edge types (tree, forward, back, cross), and the block collapses tree and forward into one case without noting this is a deliberate grouping, which is acceptable, but saying 'exactly three cases' when the standard classification has four named types could mislead.

The worked example assigns post(C)=7, but if DFS starts at A, visits B before C, and the graph is A→B→D and A→C→D, then after finishing D (post=4) and B (post=5), DFS backtracks to A and visits C; C then visits D (already visited), so C finishes next — post(C) should be 6, not 7, and post(A) should be 7, not 8, given only 4 vertices yielding post numbers 1–8 with the described traversal order.
findings.suggested_fix: Recheck the post-number assignment: with 4 vertices the valid range is 1–8, but the specific values post(C)=7 and post(A)=8 imply a gap (6 is unused) that contradicts standard DFS counter increments; assign consistent values such as post(D)=2, post(B)=3, post(C)=5, post(A)=6 (or use the full pre/post range 1–8 with pre numbers shown).

findings.description: The display-math block showing the complexity equation is immediately followed by block f1a6a8aa which explicitly interprets and extends that equation, but the two blocks share no group_id.

The callout states 'The order in which components are discovered corresponds to a reverse topological order of the meta-graph', but since the second DFS processes vertices in decreasing post-number order from G^R (which are source-first in G), the SCCs are discovered in topological order of the meta-graph (sources before sinks), not reverse topological order.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'reverse topological order of the meta-graph' to 'topological order of the meta-graph' (sources discovered first, sinks last).

findings.description: The paragraph states 'Because the SCCs are discovered in sink-first order, they appear in reverse topological order of the meta-graph', but the second DFS on G starts from the highest post-number vertex (which is in a source SCC of G), so SCCs are discovered source-first, corresponding to topological order, not reverse topological order.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'sink-first order' to 'source-first order' and 'reverse topological order' to 'topological order'.

findings.description: The recurrence uses a max over predecessors, but the base case (no valid predecessor) is not embedded in the formula; more importantly, the display formula omits the base case entirely and could be read as undefined when the set is empty — however the real issue is that the formula as written implies L[k] = 1 + max{...} always, whereas the correct statement is L[k] = 1 when no predecessor exists, not 1 + max of an empty set.

The correctness argument states variables set to true were forced by implications starting from 'unconditionally-true clauses (empty-premise implications)' but does not clarify that forced variables can also be forced transitively through chains of already-forced variables, which is the general case and may confuse learners.
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify that the chain can be transitive: a variable forced true by one implication can in turn force others, and any satisfying assignment must follow the same chain.

findings.description: The unsatisfiable example paragraph describes a specific modified formula and its step-by-step outcome but shares no group_id with the display-math block showing the original formula or any companion math block, leaving the modified formula's clauses implicit and unlinked.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a display-math block showing the augmented formula (with ⇒z added) and assign it the same group_id as the prose paragraph describing the unsatisfiable trace.

findings.description: The claim that each pivot takes O(mn) time is incorrect; a single pivot row-reduces m+1 rows each of length n+m, so the cost is O(m(n+m)) or simply O(mn) only when n dominates m, but more precisely it is O(m·(n+m)); stating O(mn) is a common simplification but the number of vertices bound given is C(m+n, n), which counts bases of an LP with m constraints and n variables — this is the correct combinatorial bound, so no error there.

The paragraph describes the feasible region as a convex polygon in the first quadrant bounded by two resource constraints, a geometric object that warrants a function2d plot of the constraint boundary lines, but no plot accompanies this description at this point in the topic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a function2d plot showing the two constraint lines (x_2 = (24 - 6x_1)/4 and x_2 = (6 - x_1)/2) over x in [0,5] to visualize the feasible polygon.

findings.description: The sentence 'The power of LP lies in its generality: scheduling, resource allocation, network flow, and many combinatorial problems can all be cast as LPs, and the simplex method solves them efficiently in practice' is a boilerplate applications gloss that adds no specific content to the topic.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove or replace with a concrete statement about what the topic will cover.

findings.description: The display-math block proving B[i+1] >= B[i] is immediately followed by paragraph 8f9d00fe that explicitly interprets that inequality and draws conclusions about fixed-point search direction, but the two blocks share no group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks 9efbb7fb and 8f9d00fe.

findings.description: The display-math block showing the rotated array example is immediately followed by paragraph 241c9b51 that explicitly interprets the labeling and identifies k and the minimum index, but the two blocks share no group_id.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the same group_id to blocks 2da0bc4c and 241c9b51.
